sections.title: 吗 (ma) Questions — Yes/No Questions
sections.explanation: The simplest way to form a yes/no question in Chinese is to add the particle 吗 (ma) at the end of a statement. The word order stays exactly the same — no inversion, no auxiliary verb. Statement: 你喜欢咖啡。(You like coffee.) → Question: 你喜欢咖啡吗？(Do you like coffee?) To answer, repeat the verb for yes or negate it for no: 喜欢 (yes, I like it) / 不喜欢 (no, I don't like it). This pattern works with any statement: just add 吗 at the end.
sections.tip: Think of 吗 as a spoken question mark. The sentence structure never changes — 吗 does all the work of turning a statement into a question.

exercises.prompt: How do you turn '她是老师' (She is a teacher) into a yes/no question?
exercises.explanation: Add 吗 at the end. Word order does not change: 她是老师吗？
Add 吗 at the end of the statement to form a yes/no question: 你喝茶吗？

exercises.prompt: Someone asks 你是中国人吗？How would you answer 'No, I'm not'?
exercises.explanation: To answer 'no', negate the verb: 不是 (am not). You do not use 吗 in the answer.

sections.title: Question Words — Who, What, Where, When, Why, How
sections.explanation: Chinese question words go in the same position as the answer would occupy — there is no special word order for questions. Key question words: 什么 (shénme, what), 谁 (shéi, who), 哪里/哪儿 (nǎlǐ/nǎr, where), 什么时候 (shénme shíhòu, when), 为什么 (wèishénme, why), 怎么 (zěnme, how), 几 (jǐ, how many, for small numbers), 多少 (duōshǎo, how many/how much, for larger amounts). Example: 你吃什么？(What do you eat?) — 什么 sits where the object would be. Do NOT add 吗 to questions that already use a question word.
sections.tip: Place the question word exactly where the answer would go. 你叫什么名字？→ 我叫小明。The question word 什么 fills the same slot as the answer 小明.

exercises.prompt: How do you ask 'What do you want to eat?' in Chinese?
exercises.explanation: 什么 goes where the answer would be (the object position). Do not add 吗 — the question word already makes it a question.

exercises.prompt: 你___学中文？(Why do you study Chinese?)
exercises.explanation: 为什么 (wèishénme) means 'why' and goes before the verb: 你为什么学中文？

exercises.prompt: Match each question word to its meaning:
exercises.explanation: 什么 = what, 谁 = who, 哪里 = where, 怎么 = how. These are the most common Chinese question words.

sections.title: A-not-A Questions — Verb不Verb Pattern
sections.explanation: Another way to ask yes/no questions is the A-not-A pattern: repeat the verb (or adjective) with 不 (bù) in between. This offers a choice between the positive and negative form. 你去不去？(Are you going or not?) = Do you want to go? 你是不是学生？(Are you or are you not a student?) This is slightly more direct than 吗 questions. For verbs with two syllables, you can shorten by only repeating the first syllable: 你喜不喜欢？or 你喜欢不喜欢？ For past actions with 有 (yǒu), use 有没有 instead of 有不有.
sections.tip: A-not-A is like asking 'do you or don't you?' in one compact phrase. Never combine A-not-A with 吗 — they are two separate question strategies.

examples.pinyin: Nǐ shì bú shì Zhōngguó rén?

exercises.prompt: Convert '你喝咖啡吗？' to an A-not-A question:
exercises.explanation: Repeat the verb with 不 in between: 喝不喝. The object goes after: 你喝不喝咖啡？

exercises.prompt: 你___学生？(Are you a student or not?) — use A-not-A with 是
exercises.hint: Verb + 不 + Verb pattern with 是
exercises.explanation: A-not-A with 是: 是不是. Full sentence: 你是不是学生？

exercises.prompt: Why do we say 有没有 instead of 有不有?
options: There is no reason, both work
有 is negated with 没, not 不
exercises.explanation: The verb 有 is always negated with 没 (méi), never with 不. So the A-not-A form is 有没有.

sections.title: 呢 (ne) Questions — Follow-up and "What about...?"
sections.explanation: The particle 呢 (ne) is used to ask follow-up questions meaning 'What about...?' or 'And you?' after a topic has already been introduced. It is short and conversational. A: 我很好。你呢？(I'm fine. And you?) It can also be used after a noun or pronoun to ask about its status or whereabouts: 我的书呢？(What about my book? / Where is my book?) 呢 cannot start a new topic on its own — there must be prior context or an obvious shared situation.
sections.tip: 呢 is a conversational shortcut. It piggybacks on the previous context so you don't have to repeat the whole question. Think of it as '...and [this one]?'

examples.pinyin: Wǒ shì Měiguó rén. Nǐ ne?

examples.en: The coffee is delicious. What about the tea?

examples.pinyin: Tā qù le Běijīng. Tā ne?
examples.en: He went to Beijing. What about her?

exercises.prompt: A says: 我喜欢猫。How do you ask 'What about you?' using 呢?
exercises.explanation: 你呢？means 'And you? / What about you?' — it reuses the context from the previous statement.
我的书呢？uses 呢 to ask about the status or whereabouts of something already known to both speakers.

exercises.prompt: What is the main difference between 吗 and 呢?
options: 呢 is more formal than 吗
吗 forms a full yes/no question; 呢 asks a follow-up based on prior context
exercises.explanation: 吗 turns any statement into a complete yes/no question. 呢 is a follow-up particle that relies on context — it means 'and...?' or 'what about...?'
